content.content:
vc-x1 0.68.0

Dual-repo commit+push+finalize in one resumable command.

Collapses today's manual Commit-Push-Finalize Flow into a
single subcommand with two interactive approval gates and a
state machine with persistent progress so interruptions can
resume without re-doing completed stages.

Stages: preflight (fmt/clippy/test) → review (approve diff)
→ message ($EDITOR / --title+--body, approve text) →
commit-app → commit-claude (skipped if clean) → bookmark-set
(app → <bookmark>, session → main) → push-app →
finalize-claude. Failures in commit-app / commit-claude /
bookmark-set roll both repos back via
`jj op restore` to the snapshot recorded before commit-app.
After push-app succeeds the remote boundary is crossed and
recovery is forward-only.

Non-interactive use: pass both --title and --body plus --yes
to skip the review gate. Saved state carries title/body
across resumes so only the first invocation needs them.

Usage: vc-x1 push [OPTIONS] [BOOKMARK]

Arguments:
  [BOOKMARK]
          Bookmark to advance in the code repo (positional form of
          `--bookmark`). The session repo always advances `main`.
          
          Accepting a positional lets the common case read as `vc-x1 push main`
          without the `--bookmark` ceremony; `--bookmark` is kept as an alias
          for scripts and for composition with other args. The two forms
          conflict if both supplied.

Options:
      --bookmark <BOOKMARK>
          Bookmark to advance in the code repo (flag form; see positional)

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --restart
          Clear any saved state file and start from stage 1

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --from <STAGE>
          Explicit stage to jump to (advanced / debug use)

          Possible values:
          - preflight:       Run fmt / clippy / test / install / retest
          - review:          Present diff for the first approval gate
          - message:         Compose / edit the commit message; present for
            second gate
          - commit-app:      Commit the app repo
          - commit-claude:   Commit the `.claude` session repo (skipped if
            empty)
          - bookmark-set:    Advance each repo's bookmark to its `@-`: app →
            `<bookmark>`, session → `main`
          - push-app:        `jj git push --bookmark <b> -R .`
          - finalize-claude: `vc-x1 finalize --repo .claude --squash --push main
            ...`

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

      --step
          Pause between every stage for an interactive approval gate

      --status
          Print where the saved state thinks we are and exit

      --recheck
          Re-run `preflight` even on resume (default: skip if last run
          succeeded)

      --no-finalize
          Stop before `finalize-claude` so it can be run manually

      --dry-run
          Print the exact commands for every stage without side effects

      --title <STR>
          Commit title (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

      --body <STR>
          Commit body (skip `$EDITOR` for the message stage)

  -y, --yes
          Auto-approve interactive prompts (review gate, message-edit
          confirmation). Required when `--title` / `--body` aren't both supplied
          in non-interactive contexts

  -h, --help
          Print help (see a summary with '-h')

Here's exactly where things stand — everything is committed locally; only the network push is blocked:

- **App repo** — commit `8c584e94` *"chore: scaffold crate, open no_std core cycle"* is made; `main` (local) points at it. `main@origin` is still at Initial commit.
- **`.claude` repo** — its paired commit `3cd5f418` is made; `main` (local) ahead of origin.
- **Saved state** — `vc-x1 push` recorded `stage=push-app`, so it will **resume from the push** (no re-commit, no re-review) on the next invocation. `commit-app` → `commit-claude` → `bookmark-set` are done; `push-app` and `finalize-claude` remain.

The wall is git credentials: the remote is HTTPS (`https://git@github.com/winksaville/tprobe`), and this sandboxed shell can't answer git's interactive password prompt, so there's no token/helper to authenticate with.

Since the state machine resumes cleanly, the simplest fix is for **you to run the resume yourself**, where your credential helper / SSH agent is available:

```
! vc-x1 push main
```

That picks up at `push-app`, pushes both repos, and runs `finalize-claude` — completing the cycle.

Alternatives if you'd rather I drive it:
- **Switch the remote to SSH** (e.g. `git@github.com:winksaville/tprobe.git`) — tell me to, and I'll update both repos' `origin` and resume `vc-x1 push main`. (I'd need SSH auth to github to actually work from here, which the sandbox may still block.)
- **Abort and roll back** the local commits if you'd rather not push at all right now — say so and I'll `jj op restore` both repos to the pre-commit snapshot.

How would you like to proceed? (I've stopped here — no further action until you say.)

Confirmed — both of tprobe's remotes are HTTPS, both of zc-msg-x1's are SSH. Here are my thoughts (no changes made):

## The real root cause is the remote URL, not the permissions

| | tprobe | zc-msg-x1 |
|---|---|---|
| app `origin` | `https://git@github.com/…` | `git@github.com:…` (SSH) |
| `.claude` `origin` | `https://git@github.com/…` | `git@github.com:…` (SSH) |

HTTPS auth needs a password/token from a credential helper or a TTY prompt. In this sandboxed shell there's no TTY, so git bails with exactly what we saw: `could not read Password … No such device or address`. SSH auth, by contrast, is non-interactive — it uses the agent socket. And that agent is **already present in this session**: `SSH_AUTH_SOCK` is set to `/run/user/1000/gnupg/S.gpg-agent.ssh` (gpg-agent doubling as ssh-agent), and the socket exists. So zc-msg-x1 pushed fine because SSH + agent "just works" here; tprobe can't because HTTPS has no non-interactive credential path.

## What the settings.local.json lines actually are

Of the five lines zc-msg-x1 has that tprobe lacks, only two touch auth:

- `Bash(SSH_AUTH_SOCK=/run/user/1000/gnupg/S.gpg-agent.ssh ssh-add -l)` — lets the bot *check* which keys the agent holds.
- `Read(//run/user/1000/gnupg/**)` — lets the bot read the gnupg socket dir.

These are **debugging conveniences** from when you diagnosed SSH auth in that repo — they suppress Claude's permission prompt for those specific commands. They are *not* the mechanism that made the push succeed. Copying them into `~/.claude/settings.json` will not fix an HTTPS remote — a permission allow-list entry doesn't change git's transport, set an env var, or provide a credential. The other three (`WebFetch(nnethercote)`, `Skill(update-config)`, `Bash(echo "exit=$?")`) are entirely incidental to that session — `nnethercote` is just the perf-book domain from design.md's reference [1].

The `Skill(update-config)` entry is a tell, though: it means in zc-msg-x1 you used the **update-config skill** to edit settings — which is the right tool for settings/permission changes.

## What I'd actually recommend (for when you want changes)

1. **The fix**: switch both of tprobe's remotes from HTTPS to SSH, matching zc-msg-x1 —
   - `origin` → `git@github.com:winksaville/tprobe.git`
   - `.claude` `origin` → `git@github.com:winksaville/tprobe.claude.git`
   
   Then resume `vc-x1 push main` (saved state picks up at `push-app`) and the existing agent authenticates both pushes. This is the primary change; nothing in settings is strictly required for it.

2. **Optional convenience**: if you *do* want the two SSH/gnupg permission entries so the bot can verify the agent without prompting, `~/.claude/settings.json` (user scope) is the right home — they reference machine-specific paths (`/run/user/1000/…`) that don't belong in a project's committed `settings.json` where they'd leak to collaborators. But treat this as a nice-to-have, not the fix.

3. **Worth checking**: whichever process bootstrapped tprobe's remotes chose HTTPS (probably how it was cloned/created). If future repos should default to SSH, that's the place to correct — otherwise every new repo hits this same wall. zc-msg-x1 may have been manually re-pointed to SSH after the fact.

One caveat I can't fully verify without trying: that the sandbox permits the outbound SSH connection to github. The network allow-list does include `github.com`/`*.github.com`, and reading the agent socket isn't sandbox-denied, so I expect it to work — but the definitive test is switching the remote and resuming.

Want me to make the remote switch and resume, or hold?

content.text:
Confirmed — that's exactly it:

- Store lookup for `github.com` with **no username** → returns a credential (redacted). ✅
- Same lookup with **username `git`** → returns **nothing**. ❌

So the push died because the remote URL `https://git@github.com/…` forces git to look up a credential for user `git`, your `~/.gitcreds` holds the token under your real username (host-matched, not `git`), the lookup misses, and git falls back to a TTY prompt that can't exist here. The credential helper was never the problem — the **`git@` in the URL** is. It's the SSH username leaked into an HTTPS URL; canonical HTTPS is just `https://github.com/owner/repo`.

## So both transports can work headlessly on this box

- **SSH** — already works: `SSH_AUTH_SOCK` points at the live gpg-agent socket. (This is why zc-msg-x1, on SSH remotes, pushed fine.)
- **HTTPS** — works too, *once the URL doesn't carry the mismatched `git@`*, because `store --file ~/.gitcreds` then matches on host and supplies the token non-interactively.

Neither needs any `settings.local.json` change — those zc-msg-x1 lines were, as suspected, incidental.

## Two ways to fix HTTPS (I'd do the second)

1. **Per-repo** — strip `git@` from tprobe's two remotes:
   - `origin` → `https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe`
   - `.claude` `origin` → `https://github.com/winksaville/tprobe.claude`
   
   Fixes tprobe now; every future mis-URL'd repo hits the wall again.

2. **Global, "ideal world" — one rewrite covers all repos, present and future:**
   ```
   git config --global url."https://github.com/".insteadOf "https://git@github.com/"
   ```
   This makes git transparently rewrite any `https://git@github.com/…` remote to `https://github.com/…` before both the transfer *and* the credential lookup — so the malformed-URL class of failure just disappears, HTTPS and SSH both authenticate headlessly, and you don't have to touch each remote. It doesn't affect SSH remotes (`git@github.com:…`) at all.

## Also worth fixing at the source

Whatever bootstraps these repos is emitting `https://git@github.com/…` (both tprobe remotes have it; zc-msg-x1's are SSH). Correcting that generator — to either clean HTTPS or SSH — stops new repos inheriting the bug. That's likely a `vc-x1`/template concern to chase separately.

My recommendation: apply the **global `insteadOf`** (option 2), then resume `vc-x1 push main` — it should sail through `push-app` → `finalize-claude` on the existing stored token. Want me to apply it and resume? (That does cross the remote boundary, so I'll treat the resume as the push it is and go quiet after — just confirm.)

vc-x1 0.68.0

Squash, set bookmark, and/or push a jj repo.

Designed for the bot to atomically finalize its session repo:
--detach exits immediately, --delay waits for trailing writes,
--squash folds them in, --bookmark + --push sends it upstream.
Every flag is opt-in. See README.md for details.

Usage: vc-x1 finalize [OPTIONS]

Options:
      --repo <REPO>
          Path to jj repo
          
          [default: .]

  -V, --version
          Print the `vc-x1 X.Y.Z` banner as the first line, then continue. With
          no subcommand, prints the banner and exits.
          
          Replaces clap's auto-version (which would exit after printing): the
          banner now rides along with normal subcommand execution rather than
          gating it, so scripts can capture the version *and* the command's
          output in one invocation.

      --squash [<SOURCE,TARGET>]
          Squash SOURCE into TARGET [default: @,@-]

  -v, --verbose...
          Verbose output: -v debug, -vv trace

      --delay <DELAY>
          Seconds to wait before squashing
          
          [default: 10]

      --log <LOG>
          Log file path (captures all levels)

      --push <BOOKMARK>
          Existing bookmark to push to remote
